serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Roast Pork Belly Salad is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Singaporean hawker centre or casual dining establishment.

nutrient_source.title: Roast Pork Belly Salad — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roast Pork Belly Salad (cr_id G00010615), confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Roast Pork Belly Salad. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed salad with meat and dressing entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is predominantly fat and protein (pork belly) with minimal carbohydrate (5.9 g/100 g from sugar and dressing); the low GI reflects the fat-dominant macronutrient profile which blunts glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 200 g serving = (45 × 5.9 × 2) / 100 = 5.3 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g threshold; contributed by fish sauce, dried shrimp, and pork belly seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 1620 mg sodium, representing 70% of the 2300 mg daily limit, and the absence of dietary fibre and low potassium (64 mg/100 g) provide no compensatory cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat density (44.54 g/100 g, predominantly saturated) with zero dietary fibre impairs insulin sensitivity and promotes postprandial dyslipidaemia
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish due to its very high saturated fat content (~14 g/100 g) and caloric density (430 kcal/100 g), which worsen insulin resistance; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited to no more than 50–80 g and paired with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate glycaemic and lipid impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~14 g/100 g), very high sodium (810 mg/100 g), and high caloric density (430 kcal/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the combination of high saturated fat from pork belly, high sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp, and negligible dietary fibre creates a strongly atherogenic and hypertensive dietary pattern that is incompatible with heart-healthy eating guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory red meat (pork belly) rich in arachidonic acid, high saturated fat (~14 g/100 g), high sodium (810 mg/100 g), and nightshade ingredient (chilli) collectively trigger multiple RA hard gates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; pork belly amplifies inflammatory pathways via arachidonic acid (omega-6), chilli as a nightshade may exacerbate joint flares, and the high sodium content worsens tissue oedema — replace with anti-inflammatory proteins such as salmon or tofu and eliminate fish sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roast Pork Belly Salad (ဝက်ခေါက်ကင်သုတ်) is a Singaporean hawker dish featuring slices of roasted pork belly — prized for its crispy skin and rich, fatty layers — tossed with fresh salad greens and a pungent Southeast Asian dressing typically made from fish sauce, dried shrimp, chilli, lime juice, garlic, and sugar. The dish delivers a bold interplay of textures: crackling pork skin, tender meat, and crisp vegetables, balanced against a sharp, umami-laden dressing. Nutritionally, it is calorie-dense at 430 kcal per 100 g, dominated by fat (44.54 g/100 g) with very low protein (1.32 g/100 g) relative to its caloric load, minimal carbohydrate (5.9 g/100 g), and zero dietary fibre. Sodium is very high at 810 mg per 100 g, driven by fish sauce and dried shrimp. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 14 g per 100 g. The dish is clinically unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, high sodium, and pro-inflammatory ingredient profile. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner dish at Singaporean hawker centres and casual restaurants, and appears on menus with Burmese-language annotations reflecting the multicultural food landscape of Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010615) with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present from the primary approved source. Saturated fat estimated at ~14 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central pork belly composition data as a supplementary reference, documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (14.0 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pork belly, cooked (FDC ID 168375), as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. This is a supplementary estimate and is flagged accordingly. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific composite dish preparation, and USDA FoodData Central does not provide a sufficiently close analogue for this exact dish formulation as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for composite dishes; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — micronutrient panel not reported by HPB for this dish and no sufficiently close composite dish analogue available in USDA FoodData Central; phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — mineral panel not reported by HPB for this dish; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this dish. Limited published data for traditional preparations of complex composite dishes is the primary reason for unavailability. Sources consulted: HPB Singapore Foodo (foodo.hpb.gov.sg) and USDA FoodData Central (fdc.nal.usda.gov), both accessed 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source — confirmed zero per the canonical HPB record; this reflects the low vegetable proportion relative to the fat-dominant pork belly component in the dish as formulated. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed salad with meat and dressing entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 5. The dish name includes a Burmese-script annotation (ဝက်ခေါက်ကင်သုတ်) reflecting the multicultural food labelling context in Singapore; this is preserved in aliases. 6. is_halal set to false as pork is a primary ingredient.
